tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: ABVD x 2 cycles followed by ISRT 20 Gy

trial_data.os_data: 97.7% vs 97.5% at 5 years (30 Gy vs 20 Gy)
trial_data.pfs_data: 93.7% vs 93.2% at 5 years (30 Gy vs 20 Gy)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage IA/IIA favorable disease by GHSG criteria (non-bulky, ≤2 nodal sites, ESR <50 mm/hr, no E-lesions). 20 Gy ISRT is sufficient for patients with non-bulky stage I–IIA disease, ESR <50 mm/hr, no extralymphatic lesions, and only 1 or 2 lymph node regions involved (HODG-3). Bleomycin should be used with caution in patients >60 years (HODG-9). Consider PFTs after 4 cycles of ABVD (HODG-4). Neutropenia is not a factor for delay of treatment or reduction of dose intensity with ABVD. Routine use of growth factors is not recommended with ABVD.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: ABVD x 2 cycles followed by AVD x 4 cycles

biomarkers.requirement: Deauville score 1-3 after 2 cycles of ABVD

trial_data.n_patients: 1119 (advanced stage, 41.6% stage II with risk factors)
trial_data.pfs_data: 81% vs 79.2% at 7 years (ABVD vs AVD)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For early-stage favorable (GHSG criteria) with Deauville 1-3 after 2 cycles ABVD. Omission of bleomycin after negative PET results (Deauville 1-3) led to decreased incidence of pulmonary toxic effects compared to continued ABVD (HODG-7D). Also adapted from CALGB 50604 trial for Deauville 3 response only; outcomes for patients with Deauville 3 response were suboptimal (HODG-5).

biomarkers.requirement: Deauville score 1-2 after 2 cycles of ABVD

trial_data.pfs_data: 94.6% vs 90.8% at 3 years (IFRT vs observation in RAPID); 98.8% vs 85.4% at 10 years (ABVD+RT vs ABVD only in H10F)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage IA/IIA favorable disease by EORTC criteria (non-bulky, ≤3 nodal sites, ESR <50 mm/hr, no B symptoms). Total 3 cycles ABVD (1 before + 2 after interim PET). ISRT 30 Gy. The RAPID trial showed somewhat inferior PFS without RT, albeit no difference in OS. Among patients with initial disease ≥5 cm, those treated with CMT had superior EFS (HODG-5A).

biomarkers.requirement: Deauville score 1-3 after 2 cycles of ABVD

trial_data.pfs_data: 91.4% vs 86.5% at 10 years (ABVD+INRT vs ABVD only for PET-negative)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage I/II unfavorable CHL with Deauville 1-3 after 2 cycles ABVD. Total 4 cycles ABVD + ISRT 30 Gy (adapted from H10U). Also applicable for stage IA/IIA favorable by GHSG or EORTC criteria with Deauville 3 after 2 cycles ABVD (HODG-5A).

biomarkers.requirement: Deauville score 1-3 after 2 cycles of ABVD

trial_data.pfs_data: 81% vs 79.2% at 7 years (ABVD vs AVD)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For early-stage favorable (GHSG criteria) or early-stage unfavorable disease with Deauville 1-3 after 2 cycles ABVD, as a chemotherapy-alone option. Omission of bleomycin after negative PET results (Deauville 1-3) led to decreased incidence of pulmonary toxic effects. Useful when CMT is not preferred (e.g., young patients where breast/heart constraints cannot be met, or doxorubicin dose would exceed 200 mg/m2) (HODG-4).

biomarkers.requirement: Deauville score 4-5 after 2 cycles of ABVD

trial_data.pfs_data: 94.3% vs 90.9% at 4 years (BrECADD vs escalated BEACOPP)
trial_data.hr_ci: PFS HR for stage II: 0.35; stage III: 0.65; stage IV: 0.79

tier_1_regimens.notes: For early-stage favorable or unfavorable CHL with Deauville 4-5 after 2 cycles ABVD, as treatment escalation. While BrECADD has not been formally tested in this setting, its use as escalation therapy is reasonable given its improved safety profile compared to escalated BEACOPP (HODG-6). The HD21 trial demonstrated significantly lower treatment-related morbidity with BrECADD vs escalated BEACOPP (42% vs 59%; P<.0001). BrECADD eliminates bleomycin and procarbazine. PJP prophylaxis is recommended for all BV-containing regimens. Utilize with caution in those with neuropathy.

trial_data.pfs_data: 100% (concomitant) vs 98% (sequential) at 41 months

tier_1_regimens.notes: For early-stage unfavorable CHL by GHSG criteria (HODG-6A). Final analysis of the NIVAHL trial showed 100% OS in both arms, with PFS of 100% in the concomitant arm and 98% in the sequential arm. Neutropenia is not a factor for delay of treatment or reduction of dose intensity with nivolumab-AVD. In the SWOG S1826 trial, growth factor support was optional.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: BV-AVD + G-CSF x 4 cycles + ISRT 30 Gy

trial_data.pfs_data: 97.3% vs 92.6% at 2 years (BV-AVD vs ABVD)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For early-stage unfavorable CHL with supradiaphragmatic disease and ≥1 EORTC/LYSA unfavorable criterion (HODG-6A). At interim restaging following 2 cycles, 82.3% of patients in the BV-AVD arm achieved PET negativity vs 75.4% in the ABVD arm. Grade 3-4 AEs were more common in the BV-AVD arm (86% vs 69%). PJP prophylaxis is recommended for all BV-containing regimens. Utilize with caution in those with neuropathy.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: BrECADD + G-CSF x 2 cycles (Stage IIB with large mediastinal mass/extranodal)

trial_data.pfs_data: 94.3% vs 90.9% at 4 years (BrECADD vs escalated BEACOPP)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage IIB with large mediastinal mass and/or extranodal disease (HODG-6B). Adapted from HD21 trial. While BrECADD + G-CSF has not been formally tested in this specific setting, its use is reasonable given the improved safety profile vs escalated BEACOPP. PJP prophylaxis is recommended. Utilize with caution in those with neuropathy.

trial_data.os_data: 99% vs 98% at 2 years (nivolumab-AVD vs BV-AVD); OS HR 0.39 (95% CI 0.15-1.03)
trial_data.pfs_data: 92% vs 83% at 2 years; HR for disease progression or death 0.45 (95% CI 0.3-0.65)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage III-IV CHL. Category 1, preferred treatment option based on SWOG S1826 trial. Growth factor support was optional during the trial. RT was administered sparingly (0.6% with nivolumab-AVD). Neutropenia is not a factor for delay of treatment or reduction of dose intensity with nivolumab-AVD. Some patients received ISRT 30 Gy on SWOG S1826 for Deauville 4-5 (1-2 lesions), ≥30% reduction in transverse diameter, residual nodal mass ≥2.5 cm, or residual extranodal lesion >1 cm.

biomarkers.requirement: Deauville score 1-3 after 2 cycles (receive 4 cycles total); Deauville 4-5 (receive 6 cycles total)

trial_data.os_data: 98.6% vs 98.2% at 4 years (BrECADD vs escalated BEACOPP)
trial_data.pfs_data: 94.3% vs 90.9% at 4 years (BrECADD vs escalated BEACOPP)
trial_data.hr_ci: PFS HR 0.51 (PET2 negative, 4 cycles); HR 0.72 (PET2 positive, 6 cycles)

tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage III-IV CHL. Category 1, preferred treatment option based on GHSG HD21 trial. 64% of patients in both arms had negative FDG-PET/CT after cycle 2 and received 4 cycles of treatment. BrECADD was associated with significantly lower treatment-related morbidity (42% vs 59%; P<.0001). Grade 2+ peripheral neuropathy occurred in <10% of BrECADD arm; 88% had fully resolved at 1 year. Gonadal function recovery at 4 years was more common with BrECADD in females (95.3% vs 72.5%) and males (86% vs 39.2%). PJP prophylaxis is recommended for all BV-containing regimens.

trial_data.pfs_data: 82.3% vs 74.5% at 6 years; HR 0.68

tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage III-IV CHL. Category 1, useful in certain circumstances. The ECHELON-1 trial design was not PET-adapted; consequently, superiority over PET-adapted ABVD according to the RATHL study has not been established. An interim FDG-PET/CT after 2 cycles may be helpful in further defining therapy. If performing interim FDG-PET/CT before completion of 6 cycles and FDG-PET is positive (Deauville 5), conduct biopsy; if positive, change therapy. PJP prophylaxis is recommended for all BV-containing regimens. Consider ISRT to initially bulky or remaining FDG-PET-positive sites at the end of therapy.

trial_data.pfs_data: 78.2% at 7 years

tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage III-IV CHL when BV and CPI are not available or contraindicated. Omission of bleomycin after negative PET results (Deauville 1-3) led to decreased incidence of pulmonary toxic effects. If Deauville 4-5 after 2 cycles ABVD, escalation to BrECADD plus G-CSF is recommended.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: ABVD x 2 cycles then BrECADD + G-CSF x 3 cycles
tier_1_regimens.notes: For stage III-IV CHL with Deauville 4-5 after 2 cycles ABVD. The Panel recommends escalation to BrECADD plus G-CSF rather than escalated BEACOPP despite lack of formal data in this setting due to improved safety profile (HODG-7D). The value of interim FDG-PET imaging is unclear for many clinical scenarios; all measures of response should be considered in the context of management decisions.

tier_1_regimens.notes: For CHL in adults age >60 years, stage I-II favorable disease by GHSG criteria, candidate for anthracycline. Bleomycin should be used with caution as it may not be tolerated in patients >60 years, and it should not be used beyond 2 cycles (HODG-B 2 of 8). Results of the HD10 and HD13 trials suggested limited benefit in patients ≥60 years receiving >2 cycles of bleomycin.

For CHL during first trimester of pregnancy with severe symptoms or organ compromise. Consider referral to center with expertise, consider pregnancy termination and urgent treatment, or single-agent vinblastine followed by ABVD after the start of the second trimester. ABVD can be safely administered in the second and third trimesters with excellent maternal and fetal outcomes. Intensive regimens (escalated BEACOPP, BV+AVD) and RT should be avoided during pregnancy.

trial_data.orr: 96% ORR, 83% CR in Evens et al. analysis

tier_1_regimens.notes: For CHL during second or third trimester of pregnancy with severe symptoms or organ compromise. ABVD can be safely administered with excellent maternal and fetal outcomes. Work with high-risk obstetrics to avoid delivery while at nadir. Consultation with pharmacy recommended to ensure supportive medications are appropriate for pregnancy. G-CSF is category C in pregnancy. Ondansetron and metoclopramide are preferred antiemetics. Breastfeeding should be avoided in patients receiving chemotherapy postpartum.

trial_data.pfs_data: 84% FFP at 15 years for stage I; 73% for stage II (Australasian Radiation Oncology Lymphoma Group)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Preferred treatment for all patients with stage IA or contiguous stage IIA non-bulky disease. Dose: 30-36 Gy. The dose of 30 Gy is mainly used for NLPHL. ISRT fields generally include adjacent but clinically uninvolved nodes when treated with RT alone (HODG-C 2 of 16). Observation may be an option for highly selected patients with stage IA disease with a completely excised solitary lymph node.

Recommended for patients with stage IB or IIB disease, and for very rare patients presenting with stage IA or IIA bulky disease. Generally, a brief course of chemotherapy (2-4 months) would be given with RT. Rituximab and hyaluronidase human injection for subcutaneous use may be substituted for rituximab after the first full dose by IV infusion.

trial_data.pfs_data: 81% at 36 months (GHSG stage IA); median FFP 10.2 months (Stanford)
trial_data.orr: 100% ORR (86% CR, 14% PR in GHSG; 41% CR, 54% PR, 5% CRu in Stanford)

tier_1_regimens.notes: Can be used for palliation in select cases of stage IIA non-contiguous disease, or for stage III-IV disease if asymptomatic. Rituximab monotherapy is also an option for relapsed or refractory NLPHL. Rituximab maintenance for 2 years may be considered for patients treated with rituximab alone (HODG-17).

One of the most common chemotherapy regimens used at NCCN Member Institutions for NLPHL. Can be used as first-line treatment for advanced stage disease or in relapsed/refractory setting. For patients with stage III-IV disease, chemotherapy with rituximab ± ISRT is recommended.
One of the most common chemotherapy regimens used at NCCN Member Institutions for NLPHL. Low-intensity chemotherapy option for early stage disease.

tier_1_regimens.regimen_name: HDT/ASCR (High-Dose Therapy with Autologous Stem Cell Rescue) ± ISRT

trial_data.pfs_data: Significant improvement in EFS/PFS/FFTF with HDT/ASCR

tier_1_regimens.notes: Category 1 for Deauville 1-3 following second-line therapy. PET negativity prior to HDT/ASCR is preferred. Strongly consider RT for selected sites that have not been previously irradiated. In patients without prior RT, TLI may be an appropriate component of HDT. Timing of RT may vary. Maintenance BV should be considered for patients with high risk of relapse and no prior BV exposure.

drugs.name: Brentuximab vedotin
drugs.schedule: Every 3 weeks for up to 16 cycles

tier_1_regimens.notes: For patients with high risk of relapse and no prior BV exposure. High risk defined as: primary refractory disease, duration of first CR <1 year, or relapse with extranodal or advanced-stage disease. Peripheral sensory neuropathy was common but improved or resolved in majority after discontinuation. The role of maintenance BV has not been well-defined in patients who received BV prior to maintenance therapy.

tier_2_handbook.definition: Hodgkin lymphoma (HL) is an uncommon malignancy of B-cell origin. The World Health Organization (WHO) classification divides HL into two main types: classic Hodgkin lymphoma (CHL) and nodular lymphocyte-predominant Hodgkin lymphoma (NLPHL). CHL accounts for 95% of cases in Western countries, while NLPHL accounts for 5%. CHL is characterized by the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells in an inflammatory background, whereas NLPHL is characterized by the presence of lymphocytic-histiocytic cells, sometimes termed popcorn cells, and lacks Reed-Sternberg cells. The disease typically involves lymph nodes and can spread to other lymphoid tissues and organs. The staging is based on the Ann Arbor system, which divides each stage into subcategories A and B, the latter for presence of B symptoms (unexplained fevers >38°C, drenching night sweats, or weight loss >10% of body weight within 6 months of diagnosis). Patients are usually classified into three groups: early-stage favorable, early-stage unfavorable, and advanced-stage disease. [MS-2, MS-3]

subtypes.name: Classic Hodgkin Lymphoma (CHL)
subtypes.description: CHL is divided into four histologic subtypes based on the WHO classification: nodular sclerosis CHL, mixed cellularity CHL, lymphocyte-depleted CHL, and lymphocyte-rich CHL. CHL is characterized by the presence of Reed-Sternberg cells in an inflammatory background. The typical immunophenotype for CHL is CD15+, CD30+, PAX-5+ (weak); CD3-, CD20- (majority), CD45-, CD79a-. Epstein-Barr encoding region in situ hybridization (EBER-ISH) is recommended at initial diagnosis, and CHL can be EBER+ or EBER-. [HODG-1A, MS-2]
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for 95% of all Hodgkin lymphoma cases in Western countries. [MS-2]
subtypes.key_associations: Reed-Sternberg cells, inflammatory background. Nodular sclerosis subtype is common in young adults and often involves the mediastinum. Mixed cellularity is more common in older patients and those with HIV. Lymphocyte-depleted is rare and often presents with advanced disease. Lymphocyte-rich is the least common subtype. [MS-2]

subtypes.name: Nodular Lymphocyte-Predominant Hodgkin Lymphoma (NLPHL)
subtypes.description: NLPHL is characterized by an indolent course and occasional late relapse. It lacks Reed-Sternberg cells but is characterized by the presence of lymphocytic-histiocytic cells (LP or popcorn cells). The typical immunophenotype for NLPHL is CD20+, CD45+, CD79a+, BCL6+, PAX-5+; CD3-, CD15-, CD30-. EBER-ISH is negative for NLPHL. The immunoarchitectural pattern should be specified as typical (subtypes A or B) or variant (subtypes C, D, E, or F), with variant patterns associated with advanced-stage disease and a higher risk of relapse. [HODG-11, HODG-1A, MS-24]
subtypes.frequency: Accounts for 5% of all Hodgkin lymphoma cases in Western countries. [MS-2]
subtypes.key_associations: Lymphocytic-histiocytic (LP or popcorn) cells, CD20+ expression, indolent course, risk of transformation to large B-cell lymphoma, especially in patients with bulky disease, subdiaphragmatic disease, or splenic involvement. [HODG-11, MS-24]

tier_2_handbook.molecular_pathogenesis: The provided NCCN guideline does not include detailed information on specific genomic events, driver mutations, signaling pathways, or chromosomal abnormalities in Hodgkin lymphoma. The discussion focuses on clinical management, diagnosis, staging, and treatment. Therefore, this section cannot be populated from the source text.

epidemiology.incidence: In 2025, an estimated 8,720 people will be diagnosed with Hodgkin lymphoma in the United States. [MS-2]
epidemiology.mortality: In 2025, an estimated 1,150 people will die from Hodgkin lymphoma in the United States. [MS-2]
epidemiology.trends: The past few decades have seen significant progress in the management of HL, with 5-year survival rates that have been unmatched in any other cancer over the past 4 decades. HL is among the most curable of malignancies with modern treatments. [MS-2]
epidemiology.demographics: Most patients are diagnosed between ages 15 and 30 years, followed by another peak in adults aged ≥55 years. CHL in patients >60 years is associated with poorer disease outcomes and more frequent B symptoms, poor performance status, mixed cellularity histologic subtype, EBV+ disease, and medical comorbidities. [MS-2, MS-3, MS-20]

risk_factors.detail: Bimodal age distribution with peaks at 15-30 years and ≥55 years. The prognosis is worse for patients >60 years.
risk_factors.strength: Category 2A (uniform NCCN consensus based on lower-level evidence)

risk_factors.detail: EBV+ disease is more frequent in older patients and in certain CHL subtypes (e.g., mixed cellularity). EBER-ISH testing is recommended at diagnosis. [HODG-1A, MS-20]
HIV testing is recommended in the workup for HL. HL is an AIDS-defining illness. [HODG-1]
Conditions or treatments that suppress the immune system are associated with an increased risk of HL.
Selection of treatment (CMT vs. chemotherapy alone) should be based on family history of cancer or cardiac disease. [HODG-4, HODG-5A]

clinical_features.typical_presentation: Hodgkin lymphoma is an uncommon malignancy of B-cell origin with two peaks of incidence: ages 15–30 years and adults aged ≥55 years [1]. The classic presentation involves painless lymphadenopathy, most commonly in the cervical, supraclavicular, or mediastinal regions. Patients may present with B symptoms including unexplained fevers >38°C, drenching night sweats, or weight loss >10% of body weight within 6 months of diagnosis. Associated symptoms include alcohol intolerance, pruritus, fatigue, and impaired performance status. CHL accounts for 95% of Hodgkin lymphoma in Western countries, while NLPHL accounts for approximately 5% [3]. CHL is characterized by Reed-Sternberg cells in an inflammatory background, whereas NLPHL is characterized by lymphocytic-histiocytic (LP or "popcorn") cells and lacks Reed-Sternberg cells [1,3]. The majority of patients with CHL present with early-stage disease, but a significant proportion present with advanced-stage disease involving lymph node regions on both sides of the diaphragm.

symptoms.name: B symptoms
symptoms.detail: Unexplained fevers >38°C, drenching night sweats, or weight loss >10% of body weight within 6 months prior to diagnosis
symptoms.frequency: Common in advanced-stage disease; presence defines B substage (stage IB, IIB, IIIB, IIIB)

symptoms.name: Alcohol intolerance
symptoms.detail: Pain or discomfort at sites of lymphoma involvement triggered by alcohol consumption
symptoms.frequency: Less common but relatively specific to Hodgkin lymphoma

symptoms.name: Pruritus
symptoms.detail: Generalized or localized pruritus that may be severe and debilitating
symptoms.frequency: Reported in a proportion of patients at diagnosis

symptoms.name: Night sweats
symptoms.detail: Drenching sweats that may require changing bedding, distinct from mild nocturnal perspiration
symptoms.frequency: Common B symptom; defines B substage

symptoms.name: Weight loss
symptoms.detail: Unexplained weight loss >10% of body weight within 6 months of diagnosis
symptoms.frequency: Common B symptom; defines B substage

symptoms.name: Fever
symptoms.detail: Unexplained fever >38°C not attributable to infection
symptoms.frequency: Common B symptom; defines B substage

signs.name: Painless lymphadenopathy
signs.detail: Most commonly cervical, supraclavicular, or mediastinal; may be firm, non-tender, and matted
signs.detail2: Physical examination should include all lymphoid regions, spleen, and liver

signs.name: Mediastinal mass
signs.detail: May be detected on chest radiograph; bulky mediastinal disease defined as MMR >0.33 or MTR >0.35
signs.frequency: Present in a significant proportion of early-stage patients
signs.detail2: Best assessed with standard PA chest radiograph; if CXR not obtained, CT assessment uses >1/3 maximum transverse diameter of chest or >10 cm

signs.name: Hepatosplenomegaly
signs.detail: Enlargement of liver and/or spleen on physical examination or imaging
signs.frequency: Less common at presentation; splenic involvement is an E-lesion for staging
signs.detail2: NLPHL patients with splenic involvement have high risk for transformation

signs.name: Splenomegaly
signs.detail: Splenic involvement classified as E-lesion or stage IIIS depending on pattern
signs.frequency: Present in a subset of patients
signs.detail2: Associated with higher risk for transformation in NLPHL

atypical_or_missed: Presentations with isolated extranodal involvement without prominent lymphadenopathy
Large mediastinal mass causing superior vena cava syndrome or compressive symptoms
Patients >60 years more frequently present with B symptoms, poor performance status, mixed cellularity subtype, EBV+ disease, and medical comorbidities [139,140]
NLPHL may present with indolent course and late relapse; patients with bulky disease, subdiaphragmatic disease, or splenic involvement have high risk for transformation to large cell lymphoma [176]
Disease during pregnancy may present with similar symptoms but requires modified staging approach

red_flags: Unexplained fevers >38°C suggesting B symptom disease
Drenching night sweats requiring bedding change
Unexplained weight loss >10% of body weight within 6 months
Large mediastinal mass with compressive symptoms (superior vena cava syndrome, airway compromise)
Severe symptoms or organ compromise during pregnancy requiring urgent multidisciplinary management
Alcohol-induced pain at sites of lymphoma involvement (highly specific)
Severe pruritus interfering with quality of life
Bulky disease (>10 cm adenopathy or MMR >0.33/MTR >0.35)

diagnostic.rationale: Gold standard for diagnosis; provides sufficient tissue for histopathological and immunohistochemical evaluation
diagnostic.key_findings: Identification of Reed-Sternberg cells in CHL or LP/popcorn cells in NLPHL; appropriate architectural pattern classification

diagnostic.rationale: May be adequate if diagnostic when excisional biopsy is not feasible
diagnostic.key_findings: Sufficient tissue for immunohistochemistry and diagnosis when adequate

diagnostic.rationale: Generally insufficient for diagnosis alone; may be used in combination with immunohistochemistry if judged diagnostic by expert hematopathologist or cytopathologist
diagnostic.key_findings: Insufficient for routine diagnosis; footnote a clarifies FNA alone is generally insufficient

diagnostic.rationale: Essential for subtyping HL; CHL: CD15+, CD30+, PAX-5+ (weak); CD3-, CD20- (majority), CD45-, CD79a-; NLPHL: CD20+, CD45+, CD79a+, BCL6+, PAX-5+; CD3-, CD15-, CD30-
diagnostic.key_findings: Typical CHL immunophenotype and NLPHL immunophenotype as described; expanded panel (MUM-1, BOB-1, OCT-2) may be required for equivocal diagnosis

diagnostic.test: EBER-ISH (Epstein-Barr encoding region in situ hybridization)
diagnostic.rationale: Recommended at initial diagnosis; CHL is typically EBER+/- while NLPHL is EBER-
diagnostic.key_findings: Distinguishes CHL from NLPHL; informs EBV status

diagnostic.rationale: Baseline hematologic assessment; may reveal cytopenias, lymphocytopenia, or leukocytosis
diagnostic.key_findings: Baseline values for treatment monitoring; lymphocytopenia is an IPS factor

diagnostic.rationale: Prognostic factor for early-stage disease; used in unfavorable risk factor classification (≥50 mm/hr if A; ≥30 mm/hr if B)
diagnostic.key_findings: Elevated ESR is an unfavorable risk factor; used in staging and risk stratification

diagnostic.rationale: Assessment of renal and hepatic function prior to treatment; includes LDH and liver function tests
diagnostic.key_findings: Baseline organ function for treatment planning; LDH is a prognostic marker

diagnostic.rationale: Prognostic biomarker; elevated in more aggressive disease
diagnostic.key_findings: Included in comprehensive metabolic panel assessment
Baseline values for hepatic function monitoring during treatment

diagnostic.rationale: HL in HIV-positive patients requires modified staging and management approach; see NCCN Guidelines for Cancer in People with HIV
diagnostic.key_findings: HIV status informs treatment approach and prognosis

diagnostic.rationale: Encouraged for patients with risk factors or unusual disease presentations
diagnostic.key_findings: May inform treatment decisions, particularly with rituximab-containing regimens

staging.rationale: Recommended for initial staging and restaging; essential for response assessment; should be obtained no longer than 1 month prior to therapy initiation
staging.indication: All patients at diagnosis; restaging after completion of therapy; interim assessment per protocol

staging.rationale: Useful in selected cases; at minimum include areas identified as abnormal on FDG-PET/CT; CT component of conventional FDG-PET/CT is often not IV contrast-enhanced
staging.indication: When anatomical detail beyond FDG-PET/CT is needed; for RT planning; when FDG-PET/CT is unavailable or contraindicated

staging.rationale: Encouraged especially for large mediastinal masses; standard CXR is best for assessing mediastinal bulk using MMR or MTR
staging.indication: Selected cases; bulky mediastinal disease assessment

staging.rationale: Alternative staging imaging during pregnancy when CT and FDG-PET should be avoided
staging.indication: Pregnancy; when cross-sectional imaging is not feasible

staging.rationale: Alternative staging imaging during pregnancy; may be used for select sites including brain, spine, or specific extranodal sites
staging.indication: Pregnancy; select anatomical sites; when CT is contraindicated

staging.test: FDG-PET/MRI (skull base to mid-thigh) without contrast
staging.rationale: Alternative anatomical imaging modality that may be considered
staging.indication: Selected cases when combined FDG-PET/MRI is preferred over PET/CT

biomarkers.rationale: Visual scoring system for FDG-PET/CT interpretation based on uptake in involved sites relative to mediastinal blood pool and liver; scores 1-3 are considered negative, 4-5 are positive
biomarkers.when: At interim restaging and end-of-treatment response assessment

biomarkers.rationale: Seven adverse prognostic factors for stage III-IV disease: age ≥45, male sex, stage IV, albumin <4 g/dL, hemoglobin <10.5 g/dL, leukocytosis (WBC ≥15,000/mm3), lymphocytopenia (lymphocyte count <8% of WBC and/or <600/mm3)
biomarkers.when: At diagnosis for stage III-IV disease

do_not_miss: Excisional or core needle biopsy with appropriate IHC panel and EBER-ISH for definitive diagnosis
FDG-PET/CT for staging within 1 month of treatment initiation
ESR measurement at baseline for risk stratification
Assessment of B symptoms on history and physical examination
Echocardiogram or MUGA scan if anthracycline-based chemotherapy is indicated
Pulmonary function tests (PFTs including DLCO) if ABVD is being used
Fertility counseling and preservation discussion before initiating chemotherapy
Pregnancy test for those of childbearing potential prior to cytotoxic therapy
avoid: FNA alone without excisional or core biopsy for definitive diagnosis
FDG-PET for routine surveillance after completion of therapy due to risk for false positives
Bone marrow biopsy if FDG-PET/CT displays homogeneous marrow uptake pattern (assumed cytokine release) or is negative, except when unexplained cytopenias other than anemia are present
Routine bone marrow biopsy when FDG-PET/CT shows multifocal (≥3) skeletal lesions (assume marrow involvement)
CT with contrast during pregnancy when abdominal ultrasound or MRI without gadolinium is available
FDG-PET/CT during pregnancy due to fetal radiation exposure

staging.system: Ann Arbor staging system with Cotswolds modification [6,7]; each stage divided into A (no systemic symptoms) and B (presence of B symptoms: unexplained fevers >38°C, drenching night sweats, or weight loss >10% body weight within 6 months)

t_stages.description: Hodgkin lymphoma does not use a formal TNM staging system. Staging is based on the Ann Arbor classification system, which defines stage based on extent of lymph node and extranodal involvement relative to the diaphragm.

n_stages.description: Involvement of a single lymph node region (I) or localized involvement of a single extralymphatic organ or site (IE)

Involvement of two or more lymph node regions on the same side of the diaphragm (II) or localized involvement of a single associated extralymphatic organ or site and its regional lymph node(s), with or without involvement of other lymph node regions on the same side of the diaphragm (IIE). Number of lymph node regions may be indicated by subscript (e.g., II3)

Involvement of lymph node regions on both sides of the diaphragm (III), which may also be accompanied by localized involvement of an associated extranodal organ or site (IIIE), by involvement of the spleen (IIIS), or by both (IIIE+S)

Disseminated (multifocal) involvement of one or more extranodal organs, with or without associated lymph node involvement, or isolated extranodal organ involvement with distant (nonregional) nodal involvement

m_stages.description: Hodgkin lymphoma uses extranodal designations (E, S, X) rather than formal M categories. E = extranodal extension; S = splenic involvement; X = bulky disease. Stage IV represents disseminated extranodal involvement.

stage_groups.criteria: Stage I-II with no unfavorable factors: MMR ≤0.33, ≤2 nodal regions, ESR <50 mm/hr (or <30 mm/hr if B symptoms), no E-lesions, no bulky disease >10 cm
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Limited disease with favorable prognosis; amenable to abbreviated chemotherapy plus ISRT or chemotherapy alone
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 5-year PFS approximately 93-94% with 2 cycles ABVD + ISRT 20 Gy (HD16 data); 5-year OS approximately 97%

stage_groups.criteria: Stage I-II with ≤3 nodal regions involved (including mediastinum and bilateral hila as single region; infraclavicular/subpectoral combined with axilla), no bulky mediastinal disease, no ESR ≥50 mm/hr, no B symptoms, no E-lesions
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Limited disease favorable prognosis; broader favorable criteria than GHSG
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 3-year PFS approximately 94.6% with CMT (RAPID data); 5-year PFS approximately 98.8% with ABVD + INRT (H10F data)

stage_groups.criteria: Stage I-II with any unfavorable factor: B symptoms, bulky mediastinal disease (MMR >0.33 or MTR >0.35), ESR ≥50 mm/hr (or ≥30 mm/hr if B), ≥3 nodal regions (GHSG) or ≥4 nodal regions (EORTC/NCCN), E-lesions, or adenopathy >10 cm
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Limited disease with adverse prognostic features; requires more intensive treatment
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 2-year PFS approximately 97.3% with BV-AVD + ISRT (BREACH data); PFS outcomes improved with BrECADD for stage IIB with large mediastinal mass

stage_groups.criteria: Stage III: lymph node regions on both sides of the diaphragm; Stage IV: disseminated extranodal involvement. With or without bulky disease, B symptoms, or elevated IPS
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Advanced-stage disease requiring systemic chemotherapy; IPS may further stratify prognosis
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 2-year PFS approximately 92% with nivolumab-AVD (SWOG S1826); 4-year PFS approximately 94.3% with BrECADD (HD21); 6-year OS approximately 93.9% with BV-AVD (ECHELON-1)

stage_groups.criteria: Stage III-IV in patients >60 years or those unfit for intensive therapy; associated with poorer outcomes, more B symptoms, mixed cellularity histology, EBV+ disease, and medical comorbidities
stage_groups.clinical_meaning: Requires modified treatment approach with reduced toxicity regimens; limited prospective data for alternatives to standard therapies
stage_groups.five_yr_survival: 2-year PFS approximately 89% with nivolumab-AVD in patients ≥60 years (S1826 subset analysis); 2-year EFS approximately 80% with sequential BV-AVD (Evens et al)
stage_groups.treatment_intent: Curative, with goal of minimizing toxicity while maximizing efficacy

staging_pearls: The number of lymph node regions involved is indicated by subscript (e.g., II3) per Ann Arbor classification

GHSG and EORTC define lymph node regions differently from Ann Arbor sites: both bundle mediastinum and bilateral hila as single region; GHSG combines infraclavicular/subpectoral with cervical; EORTC combines infraclavicular/subpectoral with axilla
FDG-PET scans may demonstrate increased avidity in lymphoid tissue unrelated to lymphoma in persons with HIV, particularly if HIV is not well-controlled

Mediastinal bulk is best assessed with standard CXR as practice-changing studies used this modality; if CXR not obtained, CT can be used with single mass >1/3 maximum transverse diameter of chest or any mass >10 cm
FDG-PET is useful for upstaging in stage I-II disease; if FDG-PET positivity is found outside already identified disease, further clinical investigation is recommended

In most instances, if FDG-PET/CT displays homogeneous marrow uptake (thought to be secondary to cytokine release), bone marrow involvement is not assumed; if multifocal (≥3) skeletal lesions are present, marrow may be assumed to be involved
NLPHL staging is separate from CHL with different natural history and treatment approach; NLPHL has indolent course and occasional late relapse
B symptoms are defined as: unexplained fevers >38°C, drenching night sweats, or weight loss >10% of body weight within 6 months of diagnosis
Patients with bulky disease, subdiaphragmatic disease, or splenic involvement in NLPHL have high risk for initial or later transformation to large cell lymphoma
Favorable risk factors differ between GHSG and EORTC criteria, affecting classification and treatment approach for early-stage disease

prognosis.overall: Hodgkin lymphoma (HL) is among the most curable malignancies with modern treatments. The 5-year relative survival rates for patients diagnosed between 2014 and 2020 were 88.6% for all ages. Cure rates for HL have increased so markedly that overriding treatment considerations often relate to long-term toxicity. The prognosis is generally excellent for early-stage disease and remains favorable for advanced-stage disease with contemporary therapies [1].

by_stage.context: Excellent prognosis with combined modality therapy or chemotherapy alone [2].
Prognosis remains excellent with appropriate risk-adapted therapy [2].
Prognosis has significantly improved with modern regimens like nivolumab-AVD and BrECADD [3,4].
Outcomes depend on chemosensitivity, time to relapse, and ability to undergo autologous stem cell rescue (HDT/ASCR) [5].

prognostic_factors: International Prognostic Score (IPS) for advanced disease: 7 adverse factors (age ≥45, male, stage IV, albumin <4 g/dL, hemoglobin <10.5 g/dL, leukocytosis ≥15,000/mm³, lymphocytopenia <8% or <600/mm³). Each factor reduces survival by 7-8% per year [6].

Unfavorable factors for Stage I-II disease: Age ≥50 years, ESR ≥50 mm/hr (or ≥30 with B symptoms), bulky mediastinal disease (MMR >0.33 or MTR >0.35), ≥4 nodal regions (NCCN/EORTC) or ≥3 (GHSG), extranodal (E) lesion, any B symptoms [2,7].
For NLPHL: Advanced stage, age ≥45 years, low hemoglobin, and B symptoms are associated with worse overall survival [8].
prognosis.prognostic_context: Prognosis is stage-dependent, with excellent outcomes for early-stage disease. The IPS remains a validated tool for advanced disease. Treatment-related factors, such as interim PET response, increasingly guide risk-adapted therapy to optimize outcomes while minimizing long-term toxicity.

disease_related.complication: Transformation to aggressive B-cell lymphoma (e.g., diffuse large B-cell lymphoma)
disease_related.management: Biopsy recommended for suspected relapse, especially at intra-abdominal or splenic sites. Treatment per NCCN Guidelines for B-Cell Lymphomas.

disease_related.complication: B symptoms (fever, night sweats, weight loss >10%)
disease_related.management: Part of staging and prognostic assessment; treated with systemic therapy.

disease_related.complication: Large mediastinal mass with potential for airway compromise or superior vena cava syndrome
disease_related.management: Urgent systemic therapy and/or radiation; consider steroids for rapid cytoreduction.

chemotherapy: Secondary malignancies (solid tumors, leukemia, myelodysplastic syndrome): Risk is highest with extended-field RT and alkylating agents. Lung and breast cancers are most common [9,10].
Pulmonary toxicity (bleomycin): Pneumonitis, fibrosis. Risk factors: age >40, concurrent G-CSF, prior lung disease. Monitor with PFTs (DLCO) [11].
Cardiac toxicity (anthracyclines): Cardiomyopathy, congestive heart failure. Risk increases with cumulative dose. Monitor with echocardiography [12].
Infertility (alkylating agents, escalated BEACOPP): Risk of premature ovarian failure and azoospermia. Counseling and fertility preservation recommended [13].
Neuropathy (vinca alkaloids, BV): Peripheral sensory neuropathy; dose modifications or discontinuation may be required.
immunotherapy: Immune-mediated adverse events (nivolumab, pembrolizumab): Hypothyroidism, pneumonitis, hepatitis, colitis. Monitor with TFTs and clinical assessment [14].
Infusion-related reactions (rituximab): Premedicate with acetaminophen and diphenhydramine.
targeted_therapy: Brentuximab vedotin (BV): Peripheral neuropathy, neutropenia, infusion-related reactions. PJP prophylaxis recommended for all BV-containing regimens [15].
surgery: Infertility from pelvic surgery or radiation; fertility preservation should be discussed.
radiation: Secondary malignancies: Breast, lung, thyroid, gastrointestinal cancers. Risk increases linearly with dose [10].
Cardiovascular disease: Coronary artery disease, valvular heart disease, cardiomyopathy. Risk is linear with mean heart dose; even 5-15 Gy increases risk [16].
Hypothyroidism: Occurs in up to 40% of long-term survivors after neck/mediastinal RT. Lifetime screening required [17].
Pneumonitis: Risk related to mean lung dose, V20, V5. Limit MLD <13.5 Gy, V20 <20% [18].
Diabetes mellitus (pancreatic RT): Mean dose ≥21 Gy increases risk [19].
Chronic renal insufficiency (abdominal RT): Minimize V5, V10, V20 to kidneys [20].
Gonadal toxicity: Ovarian failure (mean dose <2 Gy recommended; <10 Gy acceptable); azoospermia (scatter doses as low as 0.2 Gy) [21,22].

supportive_care.overview: Supportive care is integral to HL management, focusing on mitigating treatment-related toxicity, preserving quality of life, and addressing long-term survivorship issues. A multidisciplinary approach including oncology, fertility, cardiology, and psychosocial support is essential.
supportive_care.nutritional_support: Nutritional assessment and support are recommended for patients with poor intake, significant weight loss (>10%), or ongoing cytotoxic therapy. Consult dietitian as needed.
supportive_care.anti_emetic_protocol: Regimen-specific antiemetic prophylaxis is recommended. For ABVD and similar regimens: 5-HT3 antagonist (ondansetron) +/- dexamethasone. For highly emetogenic regimens (e.g., escalated BEACOPP): 5-HT3 antagonist + NK1 receptor antagonist (aprepitant) + dexamethasone. For pregnant patients, ondansetron and metoclopramide are preferred [23].
supportive_care.gcsf_guidance: G-CSF is not recommended with ABVD due to low neutropenia risk. It is recommended with BrECADD, BV-AVD, and other regimens with high febrile neutropenia risk. Primary prophylaxis should be considered for regimens with >20% risk of febrile neutropenia [24].
supportive_care.vte_prophylaxis: Not routinely specified for HL, but consider in patients with risk factors (e.g., immobilization, central venous catheters).
supportive_care.pain_management: Pain assessment and management per NCCN Guidelines for Adult Cancer Pain. Address disease-related pain (e.g., bulky masses) and treatment-related pain (e.g., neuropathy, mucositis).
supportive_care.psychosocial_support: Distress screening with NCCN Distress Thermometer and Problem List is recommended at diagnosis and during treatment. Refer to social work, psychiatry, or supportive care services as needed [25].
supportive_care.dental_care: Oral health assessment prior to treatment, especially if RT to head/neck region. Use fluoride trays if salivary glands in RT field to prevent xerostomia and dental caries.

follow_up.post_curative_treatment: After completion of therapy, follow-up should be coordinated with the primary care physician (PCP). A treatment summary including details of RT, OAR doses, and cumulative anthracycline dose should be provided. Follow-up with an oncologist is recommended due to risk of relapse and late effects [26].
follow_up.surveillance_rationale: Follow-up aims to: (1) Detect relapse early when potentially curative salvage therapy is possible; (2) Monitor for and manage long-term treatment effects (e.g., secondary malignancies, cardiovascular disease, endocrinopathies, infertility); (3) Provide survivorship counseling [26].
late_effects_screening: Cardiovascular disease: Annual blood pressure, aggressive management of risk factors. Consider ECHO at 1-year post-anthracycline and then at 5-10 year intervals. Consider carotid US at 10-year intervals if neck RT [26,27].

Secondary malignancies: Annual breast screening (mammography + MRI) for individuals assigned female at birth with intact breast tissue who received mediastinal/axillary RT between ages 10-30 years, starting 8 years post-RT but not before age 25. Lung cancer screening per NCCN Lung Cancer Screening Guidelines for smokers/ex-smokers with thoracic RT [26].
Thyroid dysfunction: Annual TSH if thyroid was in RT field [26].
Fertility: Discuss family planning; evaluate for premature ovarian failure or gonadal dysfunction if indicated.
Psychosocial: Screen for distress, address survivorship concerns.
follow_up.recurrence_patterns: Most relapses (80-90%) occur within the first 3-5 years after treatment. Late relapses (>5 years) are uncommon but possible, especially in NLPHL. Relapse risk is higher for advanced-stage, residual PET positivity after therapy, and certain histologies (e.g., mixed cellularity) [28].

clinical_follow_up_schedule: History & physical: Every 3-6 months for 1-2 years, then every 6-12 months until year 3, then annually thereafter [26].
Annual influenza vaccine and other vaccines as clinically indicated (see NCCN Guidelines for Survivorship) [26].
imaging_strategy: Surveillance FDG-PET/CT is not recommended due to high false-positive rate [29].
Diagnostic CT (contrast-enhanced) may be obtained at 3-6 month intervals for up to 2 years as clinically indicated, or after 2 years if relapse is suspected [26].
FDG-PET/CT only indicated if evaluating for potential relapse based on symptoms or clinical findings [26].
laboratory_monitoring: CBC, ESR (if elevated at diagnosis), chemistry profile: Annually [26].
Thyroid function tests (TFTs): At least annually if thyroid was in RT field [26].
Lipid panel: Biannually for cardiovascular risk assessment [26].
Fasting glucose: Annually for diabetes screening if abdominal RT [19].
supportive_follow_up: Counseling on reproduction, health habits, cardiovascular risk reduction, breast awareness, skin cancer risk, and end-of-treatment summary [26].
Consider referral to survivorship clinic and coordination with PCP [26].
Screen for psychosocial distress using NCCN Distress Thermometer [25].

special_situations.situation: CHL in adults >60 years or unfit for intensive therapy
special_situations.key_message: Outcomes are poorer due to higher comorbidity, mixed cellularity, and EBV+ disease. Treatment must balance efficacy and toxicity.
special_situations.management: Use less intensive regimens (e.g., A(B)VD with limited bleomycin, BV-DTIC, nivolumab-AVD). ISRT or EFRT alone if systemic therapy not feasible [30,31].
special_situations.evidence: Subgroup analysis of SWOG S1826 showed superior PFS and OS with nivolumab-AVD vs. BV-AVD in patients ≥60 years [32]. Phase II studies show activity of BV-DTIC and BV-nivolumab in this population [33,34].

special_situations.key_message: Multidisciplinary management (oncology, obstetrics, neonatology) is essential. ABVD can be given in 2nd/3rd trimesters; RT and intensive regimens should be avoided.
special_situations.management: First trimester: Delay treatment if asymptomatic; consider vinblastine alone if urgent. Second/third trimester: ABVD for severe symptoms; avoid delivery during nadir. Supportive meds: ondansetron, metoclopramide [23].
special_situations.evidence: Retrospective data show excellent maternal and fetal outcomes with ABVD in 2nd/3rd trimesters [35,36].

special_situations.key_message: Indolent course; observation may be an option for stage IA with completely excised node.
special_situations.management: ISRT alone preferred for stage IA/contiguous IIA non-bulky. Observation for select stage IA. Chemotherapy + rituximab + ISRT for stage IB/IIB or bulky disease [8].
special_situations.evidence: Long-term outcomes excellent with RT alone; 5-year PFS ~90% [37].

special_situations.key_message: Higher risk of transformation to aggressive lymphoma; biopsy of subdiaphragmatic sites recommended.
special_situations.management: Chemotherapy + rituximab ± ISRT. Observation if asymptomatic. Rituximab monotherapy for palliation [8].
special_situations.evidence: Transformation risk 7% at 10 years, 30% at 20 years [38].

special_situations.situation: Primary refractory or early relapse CHL (<3 months)
special_situations.key_message: Poor prognosis; requires aggressive salvage therapy and consideration for HDT/ASCR.
special_situations.management: Second-line systemic therapy (CPI-based preferred) → restaging → HDT/ASCR if Deauville 1-3. BV maintenance post-ASCR for high-risk patients [5,39].
special_situations.evidence: HDT/ASCR superior to conventional salvage in relapsed/refractory HL [40,41].

special_situations.key_message: Better prognosis than primary refractory; similar approach to early relapse.
special_situations.management: Second-line systemic therapy → restaging → HDT/ASCR if responsive. Consider ISRT for localized relapse [5,39].
special_situations.evidence: Outcomes better with longer first remission [42].

special_situations.key_message: Indolent course may allow observation; transformation to DLBCL should be ruled out.
special_situations.management: Re-biopsy at relapse. Observation if asymptomatic. Rituximab ± chemotherapy, ISRT, or HDT/ASCR for symptomatic/high-burden disease [8].

clinical_pearls: Hodgkin lymphoma is highly curable; treatment decisions must balance cure with long-term toxicity, especially in young patients.
FDG-PET is essential for staging and response assessment using Deauville criteria (5-point scale); interim PET guides response-adapted therapy in some scenarios.
Nivolumab-AVD and BrECADD + G-CSF are now preferred first-line regimens for advanced-stage CHL, based on superior PFS in SWOG S1826 and HD21 trials, respectively [3,4].
Bleomycin pulmonary toxicity does not preclude consolidation thoracic RT; monitor with PFTs (DLCO ≥60% acceptable for bleomycin use).
PJP prophylaxis is required for all BV-containing regimens.
Surveillance FDG-PET/CT is not recommended due to high false-positive rate; clinical assessment is key.

Individuals assigned female at birth with intact breast tissue who received mediastinal/axillary RT between ages 10-30 require annual breast screening (mammography + MRI) starting 8 years post-RT but not before age 25.
NLPHL has a different natural history than CHL; transformation to aggressive lymphoma should be considered at relapse, especially with intra-abdominal or splenic disease.

key_trials.full_name: Nivolumab+AVD in Advanced-Stage Classic Hodgkin's Lymphoma
key_trials.practice_change: Established nivolumab-AVD as preferred first-line therapy for advanced-stage CHL.

key_trials.full_name: Assessing the efficacy and tolerability of PET-guided BrECADD versus eBEACOPP in advanced-stage, classical Hodgkin lymphoma
key_trials.intervention: PET2-adapted BrECADD (brentuximab vedotin, etoposide, cyclophosphamide, doxorubicin, dacarbazine, dexamethasone) + G-CSF
key_trials.practice_change: BrECADD replaced escalated BEACOPP as preferred intensive regimen due to lower toxicity and non-inferior efficacy.

key_trials.full_name: Reduced treatment intensity in patients with early-stage Hodgkin's lymphoma
key_trials.comparator: 4 cycles ABVD + 30 Gy IFRT (and other combinations)
key_trials.key_result: No significant differences among 4 arms in OS, FFTF, or PFS
key_trials.practice_change: Established 2 cycles ABVD + 20 Gy ISRT as standard for early-stage favorable HL.

key_trials.full_name: Results of a trial of PET-directed therapy for early-stage Hodgkin's lymphoma
key_trials.practice_change: Supported CMT over observation for PET-negative early-stage favorable HL, though OS not different.

key_trials.full_name: Overall survival with brentuximab vedotin in stage III or IV Hodgkin's lymphoma
key_trials.secondary_outcomes: Lower pulmonary toxicity with BV-AVD; higher peripheral neuropathy.
key_trials.practice_change: Established BV-AVD as an option for advanced-stage HL, though not PET-adapted.

key_trials.full_name: Pembrolizumab versus brentuximab vedotin in relapsed or refractory classical Hodgkin lymphoma
key_trials.population: Relapsed/refractory CHL after auto-HCT or ineligible for transplant
key_trials.practice_change: Established pembrolizumab as superior to BV in relapsed/refractory CHL.

key_trials.full_name: Brentuximab vedotin as consolidation therapy after autologous stem-cell transplantation in patients with Hodgkin's lymphoma at risk of relapse or progression
key_trials.population: High-risk CHL (primary refractory, CR1 <12 months, or extranodal/advanced relapse) post-ASCT
key_trials.practice_change: Supports BV consolidation post-ASCT for high-risk relapsed/refractory CHL.
